90
 self-esteem, whereas social stigma and discrimination can under -
mine self-worth and hinder societal participation. The biopsycho-
social model, formulated by George Engel in 1977, acknowledges 
that reactions to health and illness are influenced by the interac -
tion of biologic, psychological, and social factors. As an example, 
Fig. 5.1 provides a visual summary of the biopsychosocial conse-
quences that can follow a spinal cord injury (SCI).
As an example, SCI has biologic consequences, such as im -
paired sensation and mobility, increased spasticity, neurogenic 
bowel and bladder functioning, autonomic dysreflexia, increased 
risk for infection, fatigue, and pain. Psychological consequences 
can include changes in mood, personality, self-image, and sense of 
self-efficacy. Social consequences can include changes in financial 
and residential status; accessibility issues limiting participation in 
social and community activities; inability to participate in essen -
tial social, family, and community roles; exposure to disability 
bias; and discrimination. These biologic, psychological, and social 
consequences can combine to impact individuals’ sense of iden -
tity, well-being, purpose, values, meaning, and life satisfaction, 
which can potentially have a negative impact on their mental 
health. Patients facing acute or chronic conditions undergo a 
complex adjustment process within this framework.
71 Appreciat-
ing the psychosocial impact of acute or chronic conditions is an 
	
referrals to mental health resources for assistance in addressing 
issues interfering with rehabilitation and recovery.
• A s most individuals rely on family systems in the community, it 
is important for the rehabilitation team to address the family’s 
informational, emotional, instrumental, and training needs to 
encourage healthy caregiver coping and adjustment.
• R ehabilitation specialists can influence adjustment by monitor -
ing individuals’ recoveries in a comprehensive, multifaceted 
 approach to aid what ultimately will be a lifelong process in -
volving ongoing adaptation to dynamic circumstances, redefin-
ing identity, maintaining social connection, and finding new 
sources of meaning and fulfillment.
• Disabilit y or illness creates psychological challenges for individu -
als that can put them at risk for psychopathology, including mood 
disorders, posttraumatic stress disorder, substance abuse, and in-
creased suicide risk that can impact rehabilitation outcomes.
• A biopsy chosocial approach is needed to understand and 
 support the individual’s adjustment process and address the 
emotional, psychological, and social dimensions involved in a 
patient-centered approach to rehabilitation care.
• M ost individuals adjust to their new circumstances by utilizing 
adaptive coping strategies, finding meaning and purpose in re -
maining abilities, and maintaining a sense of hope that allows 
them enhanced resilience and quality of life.
• M aladjustment can manifest in a variety of ways over the 
course of recovery requiring routine thoughtful assessment and 
SUMMARY PEARLS
5
Psychological Assessment and 
Intervention in Rehabilitation
CHRISTOPHER G. CARTER AND JULIE M. LEWIS
Background
As specialists in physical medicine and rehabilitation (PM&R), 
physiatrists encounter patients facing a multitude of medical con-
ditions, from temporary to chronic, that bring unique challenges. 
These conditions may involve (1) extended or repeated medical 
procedures and hospitalizations, (2) pain, (3) disruptions in sleep, 
energy, and general well-being, and (4) changes in functional ca -
pacities and physical appearance. The psychological and emo -
tional impact of these conditions often disrupts individuals’ 
identity, as they struggle to redefine themselves in the setting of 
new limitations and capabilities. Roles within family, work, and 
social spheres must be reevaluated, challenging established norms 
and expectations. The loss of occupational roles can lead to a reas-
sessment of one’s purpose and self-worth. Injury, illness, and ac -
quired disability can significantly alter an individual’s ability to 
engage in essential roles and activities at home, work, and in the 
community, as well as their overall life trajectory, dramatically 
altering their sense of self and their relationships with others  
and the world around them.
6 Social support is crucial for psycho-
logical resilience and flourishing, whereas a lack of support can 
exacerbate feelings of isolation and despair. The quality and stabil-
ity of social relationships positively influence adjustment and

91Chapter 5  Psychological Assessment and Intervention in Rehabilitation
essential component of patient-centered care, allowing health care 
providers to support patients in moving through this adjustment 
process, promoting resilience and enhancing their psychological 
well-being and quality of life.
Stages of Recovery
As Fig. 5.2 suggests, patients in rehabilitation typically progress 
through stages of acceptance over the course of their recovery 
beginning in the initial acute care and continuing through postre-
habilitation integration into the community. Though the timeline 
and trajectory can vary widely, these phases provide a framework 
for understanding the adjustment process and the varied trajecto-
ries and timelines individuals may experience.
37 It is highly indi -
vidualized and influenced by a multitude of factors, including 
• Fig. 5.1 Biopsychosocial model of spinal cord injury. SES, Socioeconomic status. (From Budd MA, Gater Jr DR, 
Channell I. Psychosocial consequences of spinal cord injury: a narrative review. J Pers Med. 2022;12(7):1178.)
Adjustment
Acceptance
Coping
Injury/Acute Care Postacute Rehab Postrehab
• Fig. 5.2 Anticipated timeline of progression from coping to acceptance.
personality, past mental health history and experience with adver-
sity, support networks, and access to resources. Some patients may 
progress through these stages relatively smoothly, whereas others 
may experience prolonged periods of stagnation or regression. 
This process of acceptance is not necessarily linear, with patients 
often cycling through various stages multiple times as they con -
front new challenges or milestones in their journey.
Upon first learning of their injury, it is not uncommon for 
individuals to struggle to accept the reality of their situation. This 
phase may be characterized by feelings of shock, numbness, dis -
belief, and loss. At first, the focus is simply coping with the cir -
cumstances that are the challenges of the day: managing pain, 
basic care needs, the complexities of the health care system; 
 gaining a basic understanding of the illness or disability; and es -
tablishing relationships with their providers. Denial may be a 
component of coping because it makes what is otherwise over -
whelming more manageable. As individuals establish a degree of 
mastery over these basics, denial gives way to a more active en -
gagement with the challenges at hand. Negotiating this initial 
stage is crucial because it sets the foundation for subsequent 
phases of adjustment.
On transitioning from acute care to the rehabilitation setting, 
patients and their families move from the immediacy of medical 
intervention toward the longer term journey of recovery. They 
encounter a series of challenges and considerations that shape 
their adjustment trajectory. As the focus of care moves toward 
stabilization and preparation for returning to the community, the 
impact of the injury and its implications for the future become 
more apparent. Immediate evaluations by therapy professionals 
help patients understand the full impact of their injuries, which 
can be disorienting and emotionally overwhelming, as patients 
confront the reality of their condition and the uncertainties of

junio 02, 2026. Para uso personal exclusivamente. No se permiten otros usos sin autorización. Copyright ©2026. Elsevier Inc. Todos los derechos reservados.

92
SECTION 1  Evaluation
what lies ahead and grapple with the contrast between their pre -
injury abilities and current limitations. How will they be able to 
participate in essential roles and activities at home, at work, and 
in the community? Questions related to sexuality and intimate 
relationships, reactions to their condition, financial concerns, fu -
ture-related questions, support systems, and living situations are 
likely. Individuals may see their disability as a threat to their 
identity, such as the runner who is no longer able to walk or the 
lecturer who is now aphasic. They may question their purpose, 
value, or worth. They may express concerns about being a burden 
on those around them. Emotions related to dignity may arise as 
patients confront the loss of bodily autonomy and the need for 
assistance with basic activities. A lack of education about the po -
tential for rehabilitation and recovery can worsen feelings of 
hopelessness. Disability bias can make it difficult for patients and 
their families to envision a quality future, leading to withdrawal, 
reluctance to seek help, and expressions of emotional distress, 
further isolating them from available support. The etiology of the 
injury or illness can impact their reactions, whether it was caused 
by an unexpected event such as a stroke, the actions of others, or 
a result of their own decisions or actions.
Transition Through Rehabilitation Settings
As individuals with injuries or disabilities approach discharge 
from medical or rehabilitation settings, they often confront many 
fears and uncertainties. The fear of not being ready for discharge 
is a common apprehension among patients. They may not have 
achieved the hoped-for level of recovery and are worried about 
their ability to manage daily tasks or cope with challenges outside 
of the supportive rehabilitation environment. This fear is often 
compounded by concerns about the lack of around-the-clock sup-
port, as individuals confront the reality of transitioning from a 
setting where assistance and expertise are readily available to one 
where they must be more reliant on themselves and their support 
networks. This anxiety is likely compounded for patients whose 
injuries are a result of an unexpected medical event, such as a 
stroke or heart attack, or had a complicated medical course with 
setbacks or return to acute care. Family members supporting the 
individual, who may have been traumatized by the injury events 
and early hospitalization, may express their reluctance to assume 
responsibility for someone they see as vulnerable and still medi -
cally unstable. Fear of isolation can also loom large as individuals 
contemplate life outside of the rehabilitation facility. The prospect 
of returning to familiar, albeit less accessible environments can be 
daunting, particularly if individuals struggle to envision them -
selves navigating these spaces with their new limitations. Addi -
tionally, the resurgence of preexisting problems, whether physical, 
emotional, or social, can further exacerbate anxieties and doubts 
about the future. Other factors influencing adjustment include 
chronologic age, financial resources, spiritual beliefs, and access to 
support services. Employment significantly enhances self-esteem 
and psychological adjustment for individuals with disabilities. 12
During the first year after injury, it is common for patients to 
experience a variety of emotional or psychologically reactive 
symptoms related to change and loss. 69 They can be intense and 
may include sadness, grief, anger, frustration, guilt, confusion, 
fear, and feelings of being overwhelmed, as individuals struggle to 
make sense of their new reality. These are natural responses to the 
perceived threat to one’s physical and psychological sense of self 
and well-being. Sadness, depression, and anxiety may become 
more consistent, as individuals mourn the loss of their former 
selves and struggle with the challenges of navigating an uncertain 
future. Anger and acting-out behaviors may present as expressions 
of frustration and powerlessness in the face of adversity, while 
feelings of helplessness, inadequacy, and vulnerability may take a 
toll on self-esteem and confidence.65 Feelings of hopelessness and 
helplessness may trigger suicidal ideation. Individuals and their 
families are likely to go through a period of grieving during these 
early stages in response to the multiple losses associated with ill -
ness and acquired disability as they mourn the loss of what was 
and what was expected to be. There are several descriptive models 
describing grief as a progression through a series of defined stages 
or emotional phases as people grieve their losses. 42 These earlier 
theories proposed that the emotional responses were experienced 
in a stagelike manner ending with acceptance of the loss. Cur -
rently, however, it is recognized that grief is an individualized 
process that does not necessarily progress through a series of 
stages. It has been described as an “oscillation between loss-orien-
tation, marked by intrusive, painful feelings of loss and thoughts 
of what used to be, and restoration-orientation, where one has 
more hopeful feelings and a focus on adjustment to loss.”38 It may 
wax and wane over the course of an individual’s recovery and can 
be triggered in response to encountering barriers or reminders of 
loss, such as injury anniversaries, altered life events, or milestones 
in which the individual is not able to participate as expected. 13 It 
can be influenced by social support and cultural influences from 
country of origin, religion, or spirituality. 64,70 Providers can sup -
port patients through the grieving process by checking for grief, 
listening sympathetically, and talking about and normalizing the 
grieving process, seeing the patient as a whole to appreciate the 
complex biopsychosocial impact of the illness or disability.38
Mental Health Risks
People living with chronic medical conditions and disability are at 
risk for problems with mental health and substance misuse. The 
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention published a report in 
2020 indicating that adults with disabilities report experiencing 
more mental distress than those without disabilities. 15 This is 
 supported by multiple studies examining the mental health con -
sequences of a variety of disabilities. Relative to the general popu-
lation, higher rates of depression, anxiety, and posttraumatic stress 
disorder (PTSD) have been found in survivors of traumatic brain 
injury,29,31-23 SCI, 10,65 limb loss, 3,8,17,56,59 burns, 63 and stroke. 45 
People with disabilities are at 2.5 times higher risk for suicide than 
people without disability with the risk increasing with the num -
ber of disabilities.44 Rates of substance abuse have been found to 
be higher in individuals with a disability than those without. 26 
This increased risk for mental health disorders has been attributed 
to the biologic (pain, sleep, incontinence, medication effects, 
damage to emotional regulatory mechanisms, etc.), social (loss of 
social supports and subsequent isolation, social role disruption, 
family discord, financial strains, housing insecurity, ableism, so -
cial stigma, unemployment), and psychological (loss of indepen -
dence and autonomy, loss of identity and sense of self, exclusion, 
shame, sexual health, uncertainty about the future, loss of con -
trol) changes associated with the disability. 12,34,37,65 How the in -
jury occurred, the severity and visibility of the disability, death or 
injury of another associated with the injury, past history of 
trauma, socioeconomic level, level of education, litigation, poten-
tial for recovery, stability of the disability, prior personality, and 
coping strategies can impact individuals’ ability to cope and 
adapt.63,65 Cognitive abilities may be compromised because of

junio 02, 2026. Para uso personal exclusivamente. No se permiten otros usos sin autorización. Copyright ©2026. Elsevier Inc. Todos los derechos reservados.

93Chapter 5 Psychological Assessment and Intervention in Rehabilitation
acquired brain injury, delirium, medication effects, sleep depriva-
tion, pain, or mood disorders. Problems with information pro -
cessing and retention, receptive and expressive communication, 
problem solving, and emotional regulation complicate adjust -
ment and adaptation.65
Poor adjustment can take a variety of forms, negatively impact-
ing rehabilitation and quality of life. T reatment refusal, disengage-
ment, noncompliance, and premature withdrawal from treatment 
may be indications of a struggle to adapt to changed circum -
stances. Social withdrawal exacerbates loneliness and emotional 
distress. Unhealthy coping behaviors, such as substance abuse and 
self-harm, may offer temporary relief but worsen long-term ad -
justment,12 impacting rehabilitation and quality of life. Untreated 
psychological distress, including anxiety and repression, often ac -
company maladjustment and can significantly hinder rehabilita -
tion outcomes and overall quality of life by reducing functional 
gains, increasing follow-up care needs, and exacerbating medical 
comorbidities. Depression and anxiety significantly contribute 
to  the likelihood of rehospitalization and death within the first 
6 months of stroke. 19
For individuals with lower-limb amputations, social discom -
fort and body-image anxiety are associated with poorer adjust -
ment, leading to greater activity restriction, depression, and 
 anxiety. Nonadherence to treatment can result in poorer psycho -
social recovery and higher health care costs in this population. 30 
Physiatrists play a key role in this process, often encountering 
patients with suicidal behaviors and forming meaningful thera -
peutic alliances. They are frequently the first point of contact for 
patients seeking treatment for physical ailments that may be 
masking mental health symptoms. Extended treatment periods 
allow physiatrists to identify and address these concealed symp -
toms, crucial for successful rehabilitation. 37 Recognizing and ad -
dressing these indicators of maladjustment is essential. Health 
care providers and support networks should intervene early, offer-
ing mental health services, social support, and resources for cop -
ing and resilience. This proactive approach can help individuals 
navigate challenges more effectively, promoting greater well-being 
and quality of life.25,52
The majority of patients discharge home to their families in the 
community after their rehabilitation. While much of the focus of 
care is on the individual, it is important to consider the impact of 
the injury or disability on the individual’s family who are most 
typically the primary source of care and support. Disability and 
chronic illness impact the family system in much the same way as 
the individual. Families face increased physical, emotional, social, 
and financial stressors caused by decreased resources, the increased 
time and energy demands of care, and social isolation. Demands 
of care may exceed caregiver’s physical and emotional capaci -
ties.5,74 Families may bear the brunt of the survivor’s struggles 
with adjustment. 50 This is likely to be further exacerbated in 
families facing changes in cognitive abilities and emotional and 
behavioral dysregulation.75
Families may go through a grieving process with emotional reac-
tions similar to the individual’s, which may be complicated by feel-
ings of guilt or shame if they are not living up to expectations.5,28,33,41 
Caregivers are at risk for fatigue and burnout resulting in poor 
emotional and physical health as well as decreased  well-being and 
quality of life.5,16,21,41,74 Couples can experience multiple losses: a 
sense of security, plans for the future, a sense of connection, physical 
and emotional intimacy, and mutual support.21,28 The family’s abil-
ity to cope and adjust is influenced in much the same way as the 
patient’s by the presence or absence of emotional, financial, social, 
and spiritual resources; mental health history; history of trauma, 
conflict, or adversity; and reliance on positive or negative coping 
strategies. Support for family coping and adjustment is essential 
as multiple studies have established the link between family ad -
justment and patient outcome.2,5,28,33,75 Family needs for support 
can be grouped into categories: information (about the illness/
injury/disability, rehabilitation process, prognosis, resource avail -
ability), emotional (validation of feelings, inclusion in the care 
process, social connection), instrumental (identifying available 
benefits, community supports and resources, equipment acquisi -
tion, environmental modification), and training (disability/  
disease management, care needs, self-care, and positive coping 
strategies).14 Support in managing the disruptions to normal 
family functioning, training in problem-solving techniques, 
identifying and negotiating caregiver needs, along with access to 
group or peer-to-peer supports to reduce isolation is recom -
mended for caregivers.41,50,74 Family counseling and peer support 
groups can facilitate communication and foster resilience within 
relationships.67
Fortunately, most survivors adapt to their new circumstances 
and achieve an acceptable level of quality of life. Adaptation after 
SCI, as an example, is a natural process that involves both internal 
and external elements. Resilience and positive adaptation are 
common among people with SCI with about 60% reporting a 
meaningful and fulfilling life after injury. Internally, individuals 
may experience grief, loss, uncertainty, changes in thinking, and 
shifts in identity and self-efficacy. Externally, adaptation involves 
learning to address structural and systemic barriers to navigate 
environmental and social spaces with a disability. Although sig -
nificant lifestyle changes often occur within the first few years 
postinjury, adaptation continues throughout one’s life and varies 
based on culture, situation, and context.
Factors Impacting Adjustment  
and Resilience
Factors that support resilience and positive adaptation include op-
timism, positive self-esteem, strong support systems, problem-
solving skills, positive thinking, hope, perseverance, determination, 
self-efficacy, a sense of purpose, and spirituality. 65 As individuals 
continue to adjust, they integrate their disability into their sense of 
self-identity. They develop coping strategies, acquire new skills, and 
seek out resources to enhance their quality of life. This middle phase 
involves a gradual reconstruction of life goals, priorities, and aspira-
tions incorporating disability limitations, remaining strengths, and 
new skills. In the late or long-term phase, acceptance gradually 
emerges as individuals reconcile with their new reality and begin to 
find meaning and purpose amidst adversity. This phase is marked 
by accommodation and adaptation, as individuals develop coping 
strategies, harness their strengths, find new expressions of tradi -
tional values and core principles, and build resilience in the face of 
ongoing challenges. Although acceptance may signify a sense of 
peace and equilibrium, it does not preclude the possibility of emo-
tional upheaval or setbacks. With time and support, individuals 
begin to accept the reality of their disability and adapt to its 
 challenges. This phase involves redefining one’s identity and capa-
bilities, finding new ways to navigate the world and pursue mean-
ingful activities. Acceptance is not resignation to one’s condition or 
situation but a proactive stance toward living with a disability and 
connecting with remaining strengths and assets on which to build 
recovery.22,51 Achieving a balance between acknowledging reality 
and nurturing hope is a delicate yet essential aspect of successful

junio 02, 2026. Para uso personal exclusivamente. No se permiten otros usos sin autorización. Copyright ©2026. Elsevier Inc. Todos los derechos reservados.

94
SECTION 1  Evaluation
adjustment. Although it is important to confront the challenges 
and limitations imposed by injury or disability, maintaining a sense 
of hope and optimism can fuel motivation, resilience, and persever-
ance in the face of adversity.
Resilience and posttraumatic growth are two important con -
cepts that help to explain this postinjury recovery and successful 
adjustment. Resilience has variously been defined as the “process 
of, capacity for, or outcome of successful adaptation despite chal-
lenging or threatening circumstances,” “a dynamic process encom-
passing positive adaptation within the context of significant adver-
sity,” and “the capacity of individuals to cope successfully with 
significant change, adversity or risk.”24 Resilience has been associ-
ated with positive thinking and positive emotions, an active coping 
style, effective emotional self-regulation, psychological strength, 
adaptive coping, and favorable rehabilitation outcomes. 58 These 
definitions suggest a response to adversity that takes place over 
time as individuals initially cope with and then adapt to challeng-
ing circumstances. Their capacity for resilience is influenced by a 
variety of genetic, epigenetic, developmental, psychological, and 
neurochemical factors; past experience; internal and external re -
sources, such as locus of control; and social, emotional, and finan-
cial supports.73 Posttraumatic growth (PTG) was first identified as 
“the experience of positive change that occurs as a result of the 
struggle with highly challenging life crises” leading to “an increased 
appreciation of life, more meaningful interpersonal relationships, 
increased sense of personal strength, changed priorities, and a 
richer existential and spiritual life.”4 Resilient individuals tend to 
have a positive outlook on life as they engage in active problem 
solving, maintain an internal locus of control and emotional con-
nections with others, and remain optimistic about the future. 27 
Both resilience and PTG can be influenced through interventions, 
such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), mindfulness training, 
exercise, encouragement of active coping strategies, promotion of 
humor, optimism and social  connection, connecting with purpose, 
and personal values.51,73  Psychological factors, such as self-efficacy, 
mastery, purpose in life, and optimism, along with social factors, 
such as social support and social skills, are consistently associated 
with better mental health in individuals with SCI. These predictors 
of mental health are similar to those found in the general popula-
tion and in individuals with other chronic health conditions. 
Mental health problems are not inevitable, and the interplay of 
psychological and social factors plays a crucial role in both the oc-
currence and the persistence of these issues.68
Individuals employ a variety of strategies to manage distress 
and maintain psychological equilibrium. These coping behaviors 
can be classified into two broad categories: approach-oriented 
coping and avoidance-oriented coping. Approach-oriented cop -
ing strategies, such as using problem-focused coping, seeking in -
formation, reframing perspectives, and engaging in relaxation 
techniques, enhance resilience and facilitate emotional regulation 
and adjustment. Active problem-focused coping involves seeking 
solutions to practical challenges, whereas emotion-focused coping 
addresses emotional distress and helps find meaning in experi -
ences.73 Flexible, adaptive coping styles are generally associated 
with better psychological adjustment over time. Persson and 
 Ryden describe coping as a “multidimensional, fluctuating pro -
cess.”53 Effective coping involves a variety of psychologically based 
strategies that are used in different situations. These include:
• Problem-reducing actions: active efforts to reduce or circum-
vent the problems created by or associated with the disability
• Change of values: conducting an existential review, a reevalu-
ation of life and what is truly important, finding meaning 
within the disability, identifying interests less in conflict with 
the disability and more in line with remaining strengths
• Establishing social trust:  having close and supportive rela -
tionships, identifying people on whom one can rely, asking for 
help when needed, meeting new people via support or com -
munity groups
• Minimization: playing down the personal threat posed by the 
disability: “It could have been worse” or “Others have it worse”
• Self-trust: belief in one’s capacity to master challenges
Effective coping involves finding a balance between the end -
points of two dimensions: acknowledgment of reality vs. creation 
of hope and trust in oneself vs. trust in others. This balance point 
may change depending on evolving demands or changes in cir -
cumstances, learning to control what one can and letting go of 
what one cannot, and being flexible in managing the disability 
and not extreme in any direction. 54 Importantly, these concepts 
and movement on these dimensions are subject to influence by 
role models, therapy, coaching, education, and goal setting. In 
contrast, more avoidance-oriented strategies (withdrawal, denial, 
or distraction; substance use; behavioral disengagement) may 
provide temporary relief from stress and adversity but hinder re -
habilitation efforts.73 They can serve as initial coping mechanisms, 
allowing individuals to process overwhelming emotions at their 
own pace. However, reliance on avoidance-oriented coping is 
linked to poorer adjustment, especially in the first year, as it hin -
ders emotional processing, engagement in care, support seeking, 
acceptance, and growth.71 Expressions of intense emotions are not 
immediate indicators of maladjustment or psychopathology and 
should be acknowledged and validated by health care providers.30 
The use of different coping strategies can be expected to fluctuate 
over the course of recovery. Another way of measuring successful 
adjustment is psychological well-being. It is tied to a sense of 
optimism regarding the future, the belief that things can get 
 better, often in response to indications of improved physical func-
tioning. Psychological well-being has been associated with im -
proved psychosocial adjustment, ability to cope with stress, and 
decreased depression and anxiety.51 Ryff and Keyes describe psy -
chological well-being as characterized by the presence of certain 
attitudes and behaviors more than the absence of psychopathol -
ogy.61 These include:
• Self-acceptance: being able to see both one’s strengths and 
weaknesses, having a sense of purpose, having goals and objec-
tives that give life meaning and direction
• Personal growth: having a sense of personal talents and poten-
tial being realized; having positive relations with others, 
 including close, valued connections with others
• Environmental mastery: being able to manage the demands 
of everyday life
• Autonomy: having the strength to follow personal convic -
tions, having an internal locus of control, being motivated by 
self-determination
These qualities, attitudes, and skills can be encouraged or 
taught and can be a focus of rehabilitation interventions and goal 
setting.
At the onset, more avoidant strategies, such as denial and with-
drawal, may be used to bring the volume of stressors to tolerable 
levels. At the same time, with education, encouragement in ther -
apy, effective pain and sleep management, and preliminary mas -
tery, the number of stressors reduces to more tolerable levels. 
With emerging mastery, time, and emotional support, cognitive 
appraisal changes so that the stressors themselves are seen as more 
tolerable, allowing for more active strategies. As individuals begin

95Chapter 5 Psychological Assessment and Intervention in Rehabilitation
to see indications of progress and mastery over aspects of their 
experience, hopes and expectations become more realistic. Goals 
shift, for instance, from complete restoration to maximizing par -
ticipation in essential activities.53 In interviews with stroke survi -
vors, Basseotto et al. identified the importance of value-directed 
living (VDL; i.e., behaving in ways that reflect one’s values). 4 
 Injury or disability may require a review of values and priorities 
and exploring new ways of expressing those values. Greater en -
gagement in VDL has been associated with increased psychologi -
cal well-being, decreased distress, and PTG. 4 With time and 
support, overwhelming emotions diminish in frequency and in -
tensity as individuals adapt to their new reality and can begin to 
engage in more active coping strategies.12,15,65,73
Assessing Psychological Health
Identifying risk factors for psychological maladjustment is essen -
tial for implementing preventive and rehabilitative strategies. 
Monitoring mood and psychological distress is vital, as untreated 
conditions can impact pain levels, goal achievement, and overall 
life satisfaction. A review of medical and psychiatric history, in -
cluding trauma history, work and education history, family 
 history, and social determinants of health (including social, finan-
cial, and spiritual resources), are part of any standard intake or 
history and physical. This helps paint a picture of the patient as a 
person and begin to identify strengths and weaknesses that may 
support or limit the adjustment/coping process. Asking about 
past experiences with challenge and adversity can give insight into 
resilience and coping style. As mentioned earlier, individuals with 
cognitive and physical disabilities are at higher risk for developing 
psychiatric and substance use disorders.15 Because these disorders 
can emerge at any point, psychological health and well-being 
should be assessed as part of a routine medical review. The Patient 
Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) or Beck Depression Inventory are 
used frequently to screen for depression. The Generalized Anxiety 
Disorder (GAD-7) is recommended for screening for anxiety and 
the PTSD Checklist for PTSD.65 The PHQ-936 and the GAD-766 
are surveys that can be administered in a few minutes. Individuals 
are asked to identify the frequency with which symptoms of de -
pression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (GAD-7) have been present in the 
past 2 weeks as well as the extent to which identified symptoms 
interfere with their ability to work, manage things at home, or get 
along with others. Responses result in a score that can then be 
used to rate level of severity and provide comparative data to 
monitor mood across sessions. For some individuals, it can be 
helpful to ask family members to respond to the questions to 
 corroborate patient self-reports. Suicide risk increases with the 
number of disabilities, the presence of depression, a sense of bur -
densomeness, isolation, increased pain tolerance, and decreased 
fear of death. 44 Screening for suicidal ideation as part of routine 
encounters is recommended because this has been found to be 
effective in decreasing actual suicide attempts and follow-up with 
mental health care. 43 The Columbia Suicide Severity Rating 
Scale55 is a five-item questionnaire that can be used to identify the 
presence and intensity of suicidal ideation to help determine level 
of risk and need for intervention either as a general screening tool 
or in response to indications of depression. Research has identi -
fied biopsychosocial risks for suicide in these populations, includ-
ing worsening functional limitations, new illnesses, chronic pain, 
suicidal thoughts, prior attempts, mood or substance abuse disor-
ders, hopelessness, personality disorders, and prior psychiatric 
hospitalizations.12 There are a number of substance abuse  screening 
tools that can be used to identify problematic substance use, such 
as the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification T est (AUDIT), the 
Brief Michigan Alcohol Screening T est, the CAGE assessment, 
the Drug Abuse Screening T est (DAST), the Substance Abuse 
Screening Inventory, and the Short Michigan Alcohol Screening 
T est.47 The Screening, Brief Intervention and Referral to T reat -
ment is a tool that was developed by the Massachusetts Depart -
ment of Public Health Bureau of Substance Abuse Services. It uses 
the AUDIT-C and the DAST-10 to identify problematic sub -
stance use. It then provides guidance in the use of motivational 
interviewing techniques to provide education regarding the risks 
associated with the substance use followed by referral for treat -
ment as appropriate.11
A history of trauma is common in the general population. At 
the same time, patients may have had traumatic experiences in the 
course of acquiring their injuries or during their care, such as in 
the intensive care unit. These experiences can impact physical and 
mental health and interactions with caregivers. Aspects of hospi -
talization, medical care, examination, and treatment may trigger 
anxiety, reexperiencing phenomena, avoidance, noncompliance, 
or emotional or behavioral disturbances. Raja et al. recommends 
the adoption of trauma-informed care as a universal practice ap -
proach regardless of trauma history. 56 The approach involves 
routine use of universal trauma precautions where trauma history 
is unknown (patient-centered communication and care, a focus 
on increasing the patient’s sense of control and reducing anxiety, 
providing an overview of what is expected, asking about a pa -
tient’s priorities or concerns about the visit or procedure, offering 
choices). Where history of trauma is known and the patient is 
expressing anxiety during the visit or procedure, more trauma-
specific approaches are recommended (asking what can be done 
to increase patient comfort, identify specific sources of anxiety 
and have a signal that the patient can use to identify distress, ac -
knowledging concerns, and developing coping strategies the pa -
tient can use to manage discomfort). They recommend routine 
screening and referral for more trauma-focused care as needed 
along with monitoring caregivers for indications of vicarious trau-
matization and burnout.57
These psychological health tools are not intended to diagnose 
mental health or substance use disorders so much as identify when 
referral to mental health specialists may be warranted. Health care 
providers and support networks should intervene early, offering 
mental health services, social support, and resources for coping 
and resilience. This proactive approach can help individuals navi-
gate challenges more effectively, promoting greater well-being and 
quality of life. 25,52 Regular screening for poor adjustment allows 
for targeted interventions aimed at improving adaptation and 
overall well-being. By addressing these factors holistically and 
promoting social support, mental health support, empowerment, 
and accessibility, health care providers can mitigate the risk of 
maladjustment and foster resilience, well-being, and autonomy 
among individuals facing injury or disability. 68 This comprehen-
sive approach ensures the timely provision and continuous 
 support, enhancing overall quality of life and psychological well-
being. Providing access to mental health services and resources for 
social support, coping, and resilience-building allows individuals 
to navigate the challenges of injury and disability more effectively.
Psychological Interventions
Key approaches include CBT and mindfulness-based stress 
 reduction (MBSR).7,9 CBT helps manage emotional distress and

SECTION 1  Evaluation
enhances coping skills through techniques such as cognitive re -
structuring, problem solving, and assertiveness training. MBSR 
reduces stress and improves emotional regulation and resilience, 
complementing CBT’s structured approach with mindful prac -
tices.
46 Patient motivation is a crucial factor in adapting to 
 disability and managing related medical concerns. Motivational 
interviewing (MI) can help to foster patients’ engagement by 
eliciting their motivations and guiding them toward healthy be -
havior changes.
7,60 MI 61 is an interventional approach initially 
developed by Miller and Rollnick in 1991 to encourage behavior 
change that came from the substance abuse community and has 
been used to influence change in a variety of behaviors.
48 MI is a 
relational style that involves empathic listening, expressing empa-
thy for the patient’s situation and potential ambivalence around 
change, developing discrepancy between patient’s behaviors and 
goals for recovery, avoiding argumentation, rolling with resistance 
as a normal part of the change, and respecting and supporting the 
patient’s capacity for change and self-efficacy.
48,60 It engages the 
client in a conversation about behavior change, coming to an 
agreement on what behaviors to change, clarifying the client’s 
motivation for changing, and generating realistic plans that sup-
port the client’s confidence in being able to make the changes.
60 
MI has been found to be more effective than traditional advice-
giving with even brief contacts of 15 minutes with increased im-
pact with more than one encounter.
39,61
Comprehensive Care Programs
A comprehensive understanding of psychological adjustment al -
lows for tailored interventions that address the diverse needs of 
individuals navigating acceptance and healing. Recognizing the 
range of coping styles informs interventions that support indi -
viduals in building resilience and psychological well-being.
12 
 Actively involving patients and their families in decision-making 
processes ensures that care plans align with their preferences, val -
ues, and goals. Developing coordinated care plans that address 
physical, emotional, social, and vocational needs ensures that in -
terventions from different providers complement each other. 
Continuity of care is also important to support smooth transi -
tions between different care settings and maintaining consistent 
follow-up to monitor progress and adjust interventions as 
needed.
40 Availability and cost effectiveness of adaptive equip-
ment, such as lightweight wheelchairs, communication devices, 
and home adaptations, enhance participation in daily activities. 
Ensuring autonomy in daily occupations through individual-
centered adaptation and strong social networks is vital.
1 Helping 
to identify resources and opportunities for social participation, 
such as adaptive gyms and sports, accessible recreational facilities, 
peer support groups, and volunteer activities, supports social inte-
gration.
35 Fig. 5.3 reflects the multiple levels and layers beyond 
simply the individual impacted by injury and the process involved

97Chapter 5 Psychological Assessment and Intervention in Rehabilitation
in working toward adaptation. Disability or illness brings about a 
range of biologic, psychological, and social consequences that col-
lectively impact an individual’s identity, well-being, and quality of 
life. Physically, it can cause impaired sensation, mobility issues, 
and increased health risks. Psychologically, it may lead to mood 
changes, altered self-image, and challenges in maintaining self-
efficacy. Socially, it can affect financial stability, accessibility, and 
participation in community life, often exposing individuals to 
biases and discrimination. These factors combine to influence the 
individual’s sense of purpose, values, and overall life satisfaction. 
Adjusting to such conditions is a dynamic process involving 
 emotional turmoil, coping strategies, and a quest for regained 
control and meaning in life. Key factors include the nature and 
severity of the disability, preexisting mental health conditions, 
social support, financial strain, and personal coping styles. These 
elements can either facilitate or hinder adaptation, with positive 
coping strategies such as active problem solving and social support 
linked to better outcomes. Conversely, maladaptive strategies 
such as substance misuse and avoidance-coping contribute to 
psychological distress and poorer adjustment. Addressing these 
factors early on is critical in supporting individuals through the 
challenges of disability. Health care providers play a crucial role in 
supporting patients through this adjustment, considering such 
factors as personality traits, coping mechanisms, social support, 
and preexisting mental health conditions, which significantly 
impact how individuals adapt to adversity and maintain quality of 
life after injury or illness.
Addressing adjustment to disability in PM&R requires a 
 comprehensive approach that includes thorough assessments, 
motivational interviewing, multidisciplinary collaboration, and a 
team-based care model. This collaboration may involve mental 
health professionals for counseling and medication management, 
occupational and physical therapists to develop rehabilitation 
plans, social workers and case managers to assist with services and 
 resources, medical specialists for specific interventions, and nutri-
tionists to create tailored dietary plans. Comprehensive assess -
ments involve regular evaluations using various tools to gauge 
overall well-being and specific quality of life domains. Patient-
centered interviews engage patients in discussions about their 
 experiences and concerns, helping to identify personal values as 
areas needing intervention. Regular monitoring involves periodic 
reassessments to track changes in functional status, emotional 
well-being, and quality of life over time, allowing timely adjust -
ment to care plans.18 The recovery process is dynamic, with indi-
viduals cycling through various stages multiple times as they 
 encounter new challenges. Early intervention and support tai -
lored to individual needs are essential in fostering positive adapta-
tion postdisability. By focusing on these key areas, PM&R doctors 
can significantly enhance the quality of life and facilitate healthy 
adaptation for their patients and address their comprehensive 
needs. Integrated programs that provide comprehensive care and 
social support can reduce barriers and improve outcomes. 40,72
Summary
It is important to appreciate the meaning of the injury/disability 
by understanding that patients’ priorities and values can assist 
with work in balancing hope with reality, building on remaining 
strengths, and setting short-term goals that align with long-term 
values and ambition. Allow for the chance to talk about life before 
illness/injury/disability to appreciate the injury’s impact on 
 essential roles and activities.
Comprehensive intervention strategies to employ include col -
laborative goal setting, which focuses on retained strengths, re -
sources, and abilities, and encouraging a problem-solving stance 
involving information seeking and education. It is vital to encour-
age social support and involve family members, friends, and 
caregivers in education and support resources, and provide ap -
propriate information, training, and support tailored to each 
phase of recovery.14 Implement strategies that address psychologi-
cal, social, and physical needs. Identify resources both internal 
and external to support coping, adaptation, adjustment, and 
 socialization. Utilize a variety of resources, including media (first-
person narrative, YouT ube, T ed Talks), faith or spiritual perspec-
tives, adaptive sports, peer mentors, support groups, advocacy 
groups, and volunteering.20
Routine assessment of coping, suicide risk, and need for 
 psychological/emotional support is vital for individuals with 
 high-risk factors.43 Monitoring and support can in turn enhance 
engagement in rehabilitation.
CBT , MI, and cognitive reframing can help to assist patients 
in redefining priorities and finding alternative activities in line 
with remaining strengths, reinforcing a belief in one’s ability to 
manage challenges and achieve goals through active engagement 
in their care and use of adaptive coping strategies such as problem 
solving and seeking social support.
Mental health consultation with psychology/psychiatry/social 
work can address prior psychiatric history interfering with adjust-
ment, substance abuse, suicidal ideation, and problems with 
 individual and family adjustment. Goals of care can include fa -
cilitating a (nonlinear) grieving process as patients mourn the loss 
of preinjury life and expectations, emotion-regulation training, 
relaxation, mindfulness, wellness training, and learning to control 
what can be controlled.
